description: Three named speakers debating the role of AI in creative writing
source_text:
Marcus: I think AI writing tools are fundamentally changing what it means to be an author. We can't ignore that.
Sophia: I disagree. The tools change, but authorship is still a deeply human act. A hammer doesn't make you an architect.
Jordan: You're both missing the point. The question isn't whether AI changes authorship — it's whether readers will care.
Marcus: Readers already don't care. They care about the story, not the process behind it.
Sophia: That's a slippery slope. If process doesn't matter, then plagiarism doesn't matter either.
Jordan: That's a false equivalence and you know it, Sophia.
Sophia: Is it? Both involve the work reaching the page without the creative labour being fully the author's own.
Marcus: Creative labour is already distributed. Editors, researchers, ghostwriters — all of it is collaborative.
Jordan: Exactly. AI is just another collaborator in that chain.
Sophia: A collaborator with no stakes, no perspective, no lived experience to draw on.
Marcus: Neither does a thesaurus.
Jordan: Now who's making false equivalences?
Sophia: The thesaurus doesn't generate the sentence. It just expands what you already wrote.
Marcus: I'm not sure that distinction holds under serious scrutiny.
